step.title: Step 15
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 34, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the full picture. Looking at rows 44-48:
`BBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBSSSSSBBBBBBB`
- BBBBBBBBBBBBBB: cols 15-28 (wall)
- YYY: cols 29-31 (passage)
- OO: cols 32-33 (passage)
- BBBBB: cols 34-38 (wall)
- SSSSS: cols 39-43 (right square)
- BBBBBBB: cols 44-50 (wall)
So at rows 44-48, the right square is at cols 39-43. The passage to the left is cols 29-33 (YYYOO). There's a wall at cols 34-38 between the passage and the right square's chamber.
Now I understand the maze structure better:
- The maze has a central vertical corridor at cols 29-33 (rows 14-53)
- Left chamber: cols 19-23 (rows 39-58)
- Right chamber: cols 39-43 (rows 44-58)
- The left and right chambers connect to the central corridor at different heights
Looking at rows 39-43:
`BBBBSSSSSYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBBB`
- BBBB: cols 15-18 (wall)
- SSSSS: cols 19-23 (left square)
- YYYYYYY: cols 24-32 (passage)
- OOOOOOOOOO: cols 33-42 (passage)
- BBBBBBB: cols 43-50 (wall)
So at rows 39-43, the left square is at cols 19-23, and there's a wide passage from cols 24-42. The right square is at rows 44-48, cols 39-43, which is in a separate chamber.
Now I understand why the squares got stuck:
- Left square at rows 39-43: To move up, it needs rows 34-38, cols 19-23 to be clear. Looking at rows 34-38, cols 19-33 are Y (passage), so cols 19-23 should be clear. But the square didn't move up.
- Right square at rows 44-48: To move up, it needs rows 39-43, cols 39-43 to be clear. Looking at rows 39-43, cols 33-42 are O (passage), but col 43 is B (wall). So the right square can't move up because col 43 is a wall at rows 39-43.
Wait, but the left square also didn't move up. Let me re-examine. The left square is at rows 39-43, cols 19-23. To move up to rows 34-38, it needs rows 34-38, cols 19-23 to be clear. The ascii shows rows 34-38 as:
`BBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOBB`
- BBBB: cols 15-18
- YYYYYYYYYYYYY: cols 19-33 (15 Y's)
- OOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO: cols 34-49 (16 O's)
- BB: cols 50-51
So cols 19-23 are Y (passage) at rows 34-38. The left square should be able to move up. But it didn't.
Hmm, maybe the issue is that I sent 6 UP commands, but the squares only moved 3 and 2 times respectively. Let me check the animation to see what happened.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the earlier results. After the 6 UP commands:
- Left square: rows 39-43
- Right square: rows 44-48
But before the 6 UP commands, the squares were at:
- Left square: rows 54-58
- Right square: rows 54-58
So the left square moved from 54-58 to 39-43 (15 rows = 3 UP commands), and the right square moved from 54-58 to 44-48 (10 rows = 2 UP commands).
This means:
- Left square: moved 3 UPs, then got blocked for the remaining 3 UPs
- Right square: moved 2 UPs, then got blocked for the remaining 4 UPs
The left square got blocked at rows 39-43, meaning it couldn't move up to rows 34-38. But rows 34-38, cols 19-23 are Y (passage). So why is it blocked?
OH WAIT. I think I finally see the issue. The ascii I printed is from the CURRENT frame, which has the left square at rows 39-43. The ascii shows the board state WITH the squares. So when I look at rows 34-38, I see the passage colors (Y and O), which are the underlying maze colors. But the square itself is at rows 39-43, not rows 34-38.
The question is: why can't the left square move from rows 39-43 to rows 34-38? The cells at rows 34-38, cols 19-23 are Y (passage), so they should be clear.
Unless... the square doesn't move by 5 rows. Maybe it moves by 1 row per UP command? Let me re-examine the earlier results.
Actually, looking at the initial exploration:
- Initial: left at rows 49-53, right at rows 54-58
- After DOWN: left at rows 49-53 (blocked), right at rows 54-58 (blocked)
- After UP: left at rows 44-48, right at rows 49-53
So from rows 49-53, UP moved the left square to rows 44-48 (5 rows up). From rows 54-58, UP moved the right square to rows 49-53 (5 rows up). So each UP moves the square by 5 rows.
But then, from rows 54-58:
- UP 1: 49-53
- UP 2: 44-48
- UP 3: 39-43
- UP 4: 34-38 (should be OK)
- UP 5: 29-33 (should be OK)
- UP 6: 24-28 (should be OK)
The left square should be at rows 24-28 after 6 UPs, but it's at rows 39-43. This means the square got blocked after 3 UPs (at rows 39-43).
I'm confused. Let me just try moving the squares RIGHT to see if they can move horizontally, and then try to figure out the goal from there.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the squares are in separate chambers and can't reach each other. The goal might be to move them to specific target positions within their chambers, or to move them to the central corridor.
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me move the left square RIGHT to see if it can enter the central corridor.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving RIGHT to see if squares can move horizontally
# Left square at cols 19-23, rows 39-43
# Right square at cols 39-43, rows 44-48
# If I press RIGHT, both squares move right

result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Sky blue: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Sky blue: row=[39,43], col=[19,23]
  Sky blue: row=[44,48], col=[34,38]
